speaker: interviewee
text: That's me when I was 10 months old. Chubby.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Magda Hedge, uh, Mittler. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the photograph was taken in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In about 1922?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 months. Yeah. It says 10 months young. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother, who was living with us until she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was 72 when she died. A sweet old lady. And this is my oldest, only brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your grand--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very young. His name was Feri, Ferenc. He was very young when he died in the war. He disappeared without trace, and we don't even know where is he-- where was he.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was Ferenc Mittler?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was also Mittler, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother name was [INAUDIBLE] Esther is the maiden name, and Schwarcz the, married name.

speaker: interviewer
text: When would the photograph have been taken, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she could be about-- when is the date, you mean? My brother was 15 years older than me. And-- no, no, no. My sister is 15. 17 years older than me, and it could be about when he was 22, 20, 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that just be-- just before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: But before the war, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest also, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you identify the people and date the photograph, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the middle is my older sister, who's here in Australia. On this side, that's my sister who died in England after an accident. And the other side is me, in happier times. We were all together, and we had a good, sisterly relationship then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was before the war, yes. I could be about 15, 16 at the most.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you name each person for me in turn, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the middle is Gizella, which is, in English, Giselle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: My other sister was Jolan, Joli. And me, Magda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Magda. Thank you. Magda, tell me about the photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my father. My mother, my father, and me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I could be about 12, 10. Not quite 10 years old. I was a gypsy girl, by the look of it. Very skinny. My father was 48. My mother was also 48. They still had a reasonably good marriage then. And of course I didn't know anything about it. I was a happy child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what were their names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was Rose-- Rozsi. My father was Jozi, Jozsef.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mittler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph. Yeah, Mittler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the photograph-- and the photograph was taken in Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in Budapest, yes. All of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you identify the person?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my husband. Not husband yet, when this photograph was taken. I'm not sure if it wasn't still in Czechoslovakia, or was it already in Budapest? I guess it's Budapest, because he's in a leather jacket, and he must had this escaping from the solder-- from the workers' camp and he's in a soldier's uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the soldier's uniform that he-- that he used?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, it looks like to me. The hat is definitely-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was [? Bishta ?] Hedges. [? Bishta ?] was the nickname. And [? Istvan ?] in Hungarian. Steven in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph taken during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was during the war. During the war, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was very, very young then. He was 26 when we married, and we were not married when this photograph was taken. So he could be about 24, 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Vienna. Can I talk-- That was in Vienna when we arrived. And that's me, my husband, and my little boy, George. He was four years old. '51. Five. Born in '46-- no, 50. Four years old. And I think it was close to Stadtpark. It was taken in a nice day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's-- that's Magda, George, and Steven--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Stephen in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hedges in Vienna,

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1950. MH In 1950. It's not a very clear picture, but I don't have too many of them left. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Arnold Kisch, my boss, my mentor, the kindest man I ever knew, who was taking me under his wing when I was an orphan.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that he was still alive. I wasn't already in Hungary. I mean, I was for a while. We were visiting him. We-- we were in-- in contact. As a matter of fact, that ring that I'm wearing, that was my husband's, and we got the two wedding rings as a wedding present from him because we couldn't afford a proper ring. But when my husband died, I made it into my size, and I've wore it since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my hus--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you identify--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my husband's family. That is the mother, Francisca. That is my husband's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moritz Itzmor. This is the sister who was in concentration camp with her son. Also the son and the other sister, whom I don't have a picture of. It's not here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this-- this particular memorial plate says that the people who died in this area and between them, the family of his, is-- is mentioned. Should I show it? This is Le-- Leva, Levice, where he was-- my husband was born and the family. This is [INAUDIBLE] and Kalna. It's the surrounding villages where Jewish were taken and between them, the part of the family that's all in the same area in Czechoslovakia. So these were the members of your-- your husband's family that he lost--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is the tombstone of my husband's mother, and also a remembrance of Bozsike. What's written here is the daughter of-- I meant the sister of my husband. And these are the children who was also taken away-- Vera, Roli, and Agi. They were all taken away and never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably in the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- probably to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz or Dachau. Wherever, I don't know that-- I didn't know them at all. I came to the family later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as I heard from him, that was the story, and they made the memorial on the tombstone of the mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in Levice, Leva, Also in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Magda, would you like to introduce all your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have anything to--

speaker: interviewer
text: There's a microphone in front of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there's one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to introduce all the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. With pleasure. This is my sister, the only one who lives in Australia with me. The others, unfortunately, not on this world anymore. That's my daughter-in-law. My son is working, so he couldn't attend it but my daughter is Lois, from America, and she has three lovely children, my three lovely grandchildren. Nicholas is behind me. He's 28 [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. This is my niece, who is actually not my niece but she's, because I don't have a proper daughter, just a son, she's the second. She's the second. She's the second. I have two daughters. And Stephanie is my lovely granddaughter, the only one who is a granddaughter. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Magda, what's your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister is Giselle, and she's living by herself. She's old-- old, as you see, but she's still capable to go around. And she's been taking a lot of care taken for her by the Jewish community. She's going to [? Montefiore ?] home twice a week. She's taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got two sons here in Sydney.

speaker: interviewee
text: She loves--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I've got four grandchildren and I have-- sometimes see me, maybe two, three time a week because very busy. My son is lawyer. The-- but [INAUDIBLE]. And my other son, the-- my elder son. One is born before the-- before the--

speaker: interviewee
text: War.

speaker: interviewee
text: '44, '40. '40. And the other one is after the revol-- after the war. '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Magda, you were living with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't hear--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was-- Not now. It's my turn, OK? Sorry. We were living together before I married and she still had a husband and a little boy. And that little boy was like a good brother to me, because we were so much together that I was sort of taking care of him as well, while my sister was busy. And we're still in good terms [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were hiding-- and you were hiding at the-- together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were hiding together, and the oldest son also. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Where was a brother? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was told. The whole family was introduced, and everybody--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's the way, and what else can I say? That's-- that's my whole life. My family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ellen. Ellen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ellen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ellen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ellen. Oh, Ellen, the younger-- the youngest children is in the camp. Youngest child is in the camp. He left today, and unfortunately I can't show him to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would anybody else like to say anything? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think it's wonderful that the opportunity's available to-- to talk about your past and your history. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- I think it's really good because I haven't had the chance to listen to her story and I just-- that--

speaker: interviewee
text: We all hope that, when the tape arrives, everybody will listen and we know more about the family history than before. It's-- it's one important point in my life that something left behind me if I'm not dead anymore. And they know the origin.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I told everything. Everything I told.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. That's OK. Nobody [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: She likes to talk about things which you already know. Yes. I'm glad that I have a sister still.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Me, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's all that on this-- this side of the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And my niece is actually my husband's niece, but we love her dearly. She's a good soul. And the husband couldn't make it. He's not here also-- working. So the men in the family didn't turn up, only my grandson, who is also a lovely big man already. He was 21 this year, and it's a lovely age. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I never think this come again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I think we said all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Magda.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much for coming. Thank you for having me the opportunity to say everything and to introduce my family, because they are most important in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
